learning_objectives: Identify and differentiate anxiety disorders and associated clinical symptoms.
Recognize adaptive and maladaptive defense mechanisms.
Apply therapeutic interventions effectively for anxiety management.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Anxiety becomes disordered when worry or fear interferes with daily life. Nurses help patients recognize symptoms and apply coping strategies.

sections.title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
sections.content: GAD involves persistent, excessive worry. Teach relaxation techniques and evaluate the need for medication.

sections.title: Panic Disorder and Phobias
sections.content: Sudden panic attacks or intense fears of specific situations can disrupt functioning. Breathing control and gradual exposure therapy are helpful.

sections.title: Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders
sections.content: Obsessions and compulsions consume time and cause distress. Encourage therapy and medication adherence.

sections.title: Trauma-Related Anxiety
sections.content: Post-traumatic stress disorder involves re-experiencing trauma and heightened arousal. Support grounding techniques and trauma-informed care.

sections.title: Treatment Approaches
sections.content: SSRIs, benzodiazepines, and CBT remain first-line treatments. Teach about side effects and the importance of regular practice of coping skills.

sections.title: Crisis Intervention
sections.content: During severe anxiety or panic, stay with the patient, speak calmly, and reduce environmental stimuli.

sections.title: Patient Education
sections.content: Help clients identify triggers, practice relaxation exercises, and connect with support groups or counseling.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.content: With empathy and evidence-based interventions, nurses can reduce anxiety and improve quality of life.
